comments:
Summary:
This paper studies a natural and practically relevant relaxation of the classical multi-armed bandit model in which the learner may query (observe) the reward of an arm that is different from the arm she exploits in that round. The authors present a multiplicative-weights-style algorithm that uses an adaptive non-uniform querying distribution (query probability proportional to the square root of the action selection probability). They derive data-dependent regret bounds that replace the usual dependence on k (number of arms) by a quantity involving the average `1/2-norm of the action distributions, and show improved guarantees in piecewise-stationary / small-good-set stochastic settings. The paper also proves lower bounds showing (i) that decoupling can provably help in some adversarial settings (Ω(√kT ) lower bound for standard bandits vs better for decoupled algorithm for certain reward regimes), and (ii) that a fixed/uniform querying distribution cannot obtain improved guarantees in the adversarial regime. Experiments on a simulated ultra-wideband channel selection task support the claims.

Strengths:
- The problem formulation (decoupling sensing and acting) is well motivated by realistic scenarios (UWB sensing) and is modelled cleanly. 
- The algorithmic idea (choose queries non-uniformly, proportional to √p) is simple, intuitive, and theoretically justified by variance minimization.
- The paper provides a rich theoretical treatment: high-probability regret bounds (Thm.1), specialized stochastic/piecewise-stationary guarantees with improved dependence on k (Thm.2,4), extensions to tracking the best sequence (switching case) and matching lower bounds (Thm.5,6). The necessity result for adaptive querying is a compelling theoretical justification for the non-uniform query policy.
- Proof sketches and appendices appear thorough; the authors state assumptions and limitations (e.g., oblivious adversary) clearly.
- The empirical study, while preliminary, illustrates the practical promise of the approach and the adaptive nature of querying.

Weaknesses / Concerns:
- The results rely on the oblivious-adversary assumption (rewards fixed in advance). The paper acknowledges this as an open question, but many practical online settings may involve adaptive adversaries; it would be useful to discuss more concretely which parts of the analysis break and whether some weaker guarantees might be salvaged.
- The main bounds use a somewhat opaque data-dependent probability P(v,δ,µ) and the quantity 1/T ∑‖p(t)‖1/2. While the paper gives intuition and derives concrete regimes (piecewise-stationary) where these are small, readers may find it hard to assess performance without guidance on estimating/controlling these quantities in practice. The doubling trick is mentioned but not evaluated experimentally.
- Experiments are limited in scope: a single (although realistic) UWB simulation, a small k (10) and T (10k) and a small selection of baselines. In particular, more ablation would help: compare to (a) decoupled but uniform-query variant, (b) effect of choice of µ and doubling trick, (c) performance as k and T vary, and (d) performance when exploited-arm reward is additionally revealed.
- Some practical considerations are not modeled: cost of querying (if any), possible constraints correlating sensing quality with exploitation choice (the paper mentions this as future work), and the effect of noisy/biased sensing.
- Presentation: the paper is dense in places (proof sections heavy in technical detail); some readers would appreciate a more detailed discussion or intuition around the P(v,δ,µ) term and how to choose µ in practice beyond the asymptotic choice.

Suggestions for Improvement:
- Expand the empirical section: larger range of k and T, several realizations to show variance, and ablation comparing the sqrt-query distribution with uniform and other heuristics.
- Add a small subsection with practical guidance for choosing µ and for running the doubling trick, perhaps with a toy example showing how it behaves.
- Discuss more concretely the obstacles to extending results to adaptive adversaries and whether any weaker (e.g., expected regret) guarantees could be obtained.
- If possible, add a short complexity discussion (time per round) and whether sampling multiple queries per round (c>1) is straightforward in practice (authors mention it theoretically).

Overall Assessment:
This is a solid paper that addresses an important and realistic relaxation of the bandit model. The idea of decoupling exploration and exploitation and using an adaptive non-uniform query policy is both novel in this adversarial context and well justified theoretically. The combination of upper bounds, lower bounds, and experiments makes a convincing case that the approach yields provable and practical advantages in several regimes (notably piecewise-stationary stochastic/limited-good-arm settings). Limitations (oblivious adversary, limited experiments) are acknowledged and are reasonable directions for future work. I recommend acceptance and presentation (oral) at a major venue.
